items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!".

rules_checked.reasoning: The item is declared as kind 'type' and describes a concrete Swift type (Greeter) with instance methods. This matches what is actually being built — a type, not a protocol, function, or module. Kind matches actual shape.
Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift places the Greeter type inside its own Greeter module/source root, which is the natural home for the library's primary type. No cross-module misplacement.

This is a plain library type with no view layer, view-model, or data-access context in this feature; the greet logic is simple string formatting held in the type itself, not smuggled into a view or view model. No altitude violation applies.

A single new type is warranted here since this is the first and only type in a brand-new module (F-M1 introduces Greeter itself); there is no existing type to extend. Granularity (one type, two related greet methods) is appropriately scoped and cohesive (SRP).
No protocol is introduced by this item — it's a concrete type only. No speculative abstraction is present, so this rule is satisfied trivially.
Greeter (noun, capitalized type name), greet(name:) and greet() (verb-based method names, lowerCamelCase) follow Swift API Design Guidelines conventions for types and methods.
The item is a single self-contained file with no declared imports beyond standard library needs implied by string formatting; no forbidden import or reversed dependency direction is introduced.
